# mSigSDK \- browser-native client-side computation of mutation signatures

Aaron Ge1,2\*, Tongwu Zhang1, Yasmmin Côrtes Martins3, Maria Teresa Landi1, Brian Park1, Kailing Chen1, Jeya Balasubramanian1, Jonas S Almeida1  
\* age1@som.umaryland.edu  
1 \- Division of Cancer Epidemiology and Genetics, National Cancer Institute, National Institutes of Health, Maryland, USA  
2 \- University of Maryland, School of Medicine, Maryland, USA  
3 \- National Laboratory of Scientific Computing, Petrópolis, Brazil

## Abstract

#### **Background**

Mutational-signature analysis combines reference-catalog access, matrix generation, fitting or extraction, uncertainty assessment, visualization, and reporting. Several mature R and Python packages cover most of this workflow end-to-end, but each requires local installation, each uses its own input format, and recent comparative work has shown that the choice of fitting tool meaningfully affects results. Running multiple tools to triangulate findings is therefore desirable but operationally expensive. There is also, to our knowledge, no widely adopted versioned machine-readable report format that captures parameters, quality-control evidence, and provenance in a way other tools can consume.

#### **Results**

mSigSDK is a JavaScript software development kit that loads in any modern standards-compliant browser through a single dynamic module import, with no installation. **Its architecture is FAIR-aligned and serverless: public reference resources are fetched through their public APIs, while user spectra, fitted exposures, quality-control outputs, and reports are never transmitted by the SDK and computation occurs on the user's device.** Within this architecture the SDK provides **the following** integrated capabilities: orchestration of four established mutational-signature tools through a uniform adapter layer (SigProfilerAssignment, MuSiCal, deconstructSigs, and sigminer); a browser-native non-negative matrix factorization implementation for exploratory de novo extraction; a user-configurable quality-control and reporting layer; reproducible reports serialized under a **proposed, versioned** JSON Schema; and a visualization layer reusing the established mSigPortal mutational-spectrum renderer. **We additionally provide an optional strict-local mode that disables every network path capable of transmitting user-derived data, verified in testing to issue zero such requests.**

**We validated the SDK along four axes. First, a zero-install cold-start walk-through completed from browser page-load to rendered report in 4.993 s (cumulative), with most time spent importing the full SDK module graph rather than fitting. Second, in-browser adapter execution reproduced conventional local execution for all four wrapped tools on the 38-sample benchmark: deconstructSigs and sigminer through local Rscript comparators, and SigProfilerAssignment and MuSiCal through Dockerized Python comparators. Third, the SDK's internal solvers reproduced established reference implementations. Fourth, runtime benchmarks across Chrome, Edge, and Firefox showed that warm-start NNLS exposure solves for a 120-sample cohort complete in 17-25 ms median and a 300-sample, 40-signature solve in 1.65-2.88 s median; these figures measure exposure-solve scenarios only and exclude end-to-end all-sample bootstrap review.**

#### **Conclusions**

mSigSDK is a browser-native, zero-install, FAIR-aligned software development kit for mutational-signature analysis, encompassing multi-tool orchestration, exploratory extraction, configurable quality-controlled review, and proposed versioned reporting, **without the SDK transmitting user spectra, exposures, quality-control results, or reports off the device.** 

**Keywords:** mutational signatures; JavaScript; browser-native analysis; serverless; FAIR; tool orchestration; WebR; Pyodide; reproducible reporting; cancer genomics.

## Background

Mutational signatures summarize somatic mutation patterns associated with endogenous and exogenous mutagenic processes, DNA repair and replication defects, and some therapy-related exposures [\[1\], \[2\], \[3\], \[4\]](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?DtSJ9U). A complete signature analysis combines reference-catalog access, matrix generation, fitting or extraction, uncertainty assessment, visualization, and reporting. Several mature packages cover most of this workflow end-to-end: SigProfilerAssignment for known-signature assignment [\[5\]](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?9dQOHS), SigProfilerExtractor for de novo extraction [\[6\]](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?O5V1xy), MuSiCal for sparse likelihood-based refitting and discovery [\[7\]](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?tW7qxt), deconstructSigs for R-based decomposition [\[8\]](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?kemwFm), MutationalPatterns and musicatk for comprehensive R workflows [\[9\], \[10\]](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?0BSnQE), sigminer for extraction and visualization [\[11\]](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?6ZXhCZ), and mSigPortal for portal-hosted aggregate views [\[12\]](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?TJ8uwV). Each package handles its respective scope well.

Two terms recur throughout this manuscript and are worth defining. A software development kit (SDK) is a programming library that other software and analysts call directly through a documented interface, rather than a finished application with a fixed graphical interface; mSigSDK is consumed as JavaScript function calls. A signature *exposure* (or activity) is the estimated contribution of a given reference signature to a sample's observed mutation spectrum — that is, how much of the sample's mutational profile each process accounts for. We also distinguish two operations on signatures: *fitting* (also called assignment or refitting), which estimates exposures of a sample against a fixed catalog of known signatures, from *de novo extraction*, which derives signature profiles directly from a cohort without a predefined catalog. 

However, three problems remain. First, all of these packages require local installation in R, Python, or a portal session, which limits reuse and raises privacy concerns when investigators analyze sensitive sequencing data on local devices. Second, the choice of fitting tool has substantive consequences for the resulting exposures, as recent comparative work has documented [\[13\], \[14\]](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?YGxyy2); running multiple tools to triangulate results is therefore desirable but operationally expensive because each tool brings its own environment and input format, and reconciliation across tools must be done by hand. Third, to our knowledge no widely adopted versioned, machine-readable format currently exists for serializing the parameters, quality-control evidence, warnings, and provenance of a mutational-signature analysis in a way that downstream tools can consume. 

We present mSigSDK, which is a JavaScript software development kit that addresses these three problems together. It runs entirely in the browser, with no installation, **so that the SDK does not transmit user spectra or derived results off the device, and reuse is bounded only by access to a web browser. We are deliberately precise about the scope of this property below (see "Data-residency and security model"), because browser-native execution reduces but does not by itself eliminate data-exposure risk.** It orchestrates multiple established mutational-signature packages through a uniform adapter layer that runs them in-browser using WebR and Pyodide, so comparing tools is reduced to issuing parallel calls on the same input matrix rather than maintaining several local environments. And it serializes results, including parameters, quality-control evidence, warnings, recommended actions, and full provenance, under a **proposed, versioned** JSON Schema, so that complete analyses can be shared as self-describing records that other tools can read. 

The architectural choice that makes this possible is to engage public reference resources through their existing APIs and to keep computation client-side, rather than to aggregate raw data in a single backend. The reusability of data-intensive computational constructs has become a major concern [\[15\]](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?H9FRPC), and we argue that modularizing libraries around data-resource APIs offers a more durable foundation for reusable interactive analytics. Wilkinson et al.’s FAIR principles for scientific data stewardship [\[16\]](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?8r50HM) frame the web as a distributed data space served by stateless APIs, and engaging this space through client-side software development kits has previously enabled work in genomic data commons [\[17\], \[18\]](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?q7gath), population-scale GWAS analytics [\[19\]](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?uKph4n), and real-time pandemic mortality tracking [\[15\], \[20\]](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?eYyPRI). mSigSDK applies the same design pattern to mutational-signature analysis. **A consequence of this design, which we examine explicitly in the Discussion, is that result reproducibility depends on the continued availability and stability of the public APIs queried; we weigh this trade-off rather than treating the API-first approach as strictly dominant.**

Because the SDK requires no installation and runs **either** in the browser **or headlessly under Node.js (see "Headless and scripted execution")**, it is usable by anyone working with mutational signatures, including computational analysts running cohort studies, translational researchers handling restricted local data, methods developers needing a consistent comparison layer for their own outputs, and educators teaching signature interpretation without provisioning per-student computing environments. **We return to the question of which entry point suits which audience, and the limitations of the developer-console entry point for non-developers, in the Discussion.**

## Implementation

### Architecture

mSigSDK is distributed as a modular package in the standard ECMAScript module format and loads in modern standards-compliant browsers through a single dynamic import from its public endpoint, with no package manager, conda environment, or container image required (**Figure 1**). User-supplied spectra and mutation annotation format-derived matrices remain in the client runtime after import; only public-resource queries – to mSigPortal, The Cancer Genome Atlas and the Genomic Data Commons, and the UCSC Genome Browser sequence application programming interface – and externally loaded web assets cross the network. The SDK keeps two runtime tiers distinct: a native JavaScript tier for core functionality, including its own matrix conversion and non-negative matrix factorization implementations; and an optional Pyodide or WebR tier that runs compatible Python or R packages in a browser worker. Inputs may be precomputed spectra in tab-separated, SigProfiler-style, COSMIC-style, or MuSiCal-compatible formats, or mutation annotation format-like rows that the SDK converts into the standard single-base substitution, double-base substitution, and small insertion or deletion profile types. Full input specifications and conversion options are in the project documentation. 

![][image1]**Figure 1\. Architecture and data-residency boundary.** mSigSDK performs mutational signature analysis in the browser: public spectra, COSMIC catalogs, and runtime assets can be fetched, but user spectra, fitted exposures, quality-control outputs, plots, and reports stay on the device unless exported. **The figure now marks the data-residency boundary explicitly, distinguishing (i) data never transmitted by the SDK (user spectra, exposures, quality-control outputs, reports); (ii) public identifiers sent only when the user invokes a public-data fetcher (e.g. sample, gene, project, or file identifiers); and (iii) the live mutation-annotation-format context lookup, which transmits mutation coordinates to the UCSC sequence API in the default online path and is disabled under strict-local mode.** 

### **Data-residency and security model**

**Because "user data do not leave the device" is easy to over-read, we specify the data-residency and trust model precisely. We audited every network path reachable from the SDK entry point and classify them into three tiers.**

**First, data that the SDK never transmits: user spectra, fitted exposures, quality-control evidence and reporting modes, generated plots, and serialized reports. These remain in the client runtime and are written off the device only when the user explicitly exports a report.**

**Second, public identifiers transmitted only when the user invokes a public-data fetcher. Fetching public spectra, reference signatures, or expression/mutation files sends the corresponding identifiers (sample identifiers to mSigPortal; gene, project, and file identifiers to the Genomic Data Commons) in request URLs or bodies. These are identifiers for public resources the user chooses to retrieve, not the user's private genomes; every such request is unauthenticated, and no code path sets an authentication token or signs in to controlled-access data.**

**Third, mutation coordinates transmitted during online mutation-annotation-format conversion. When converting a user-supplied MAF without row-supplied or bundled context, the SDK queries the UCSC sequence API with chromosome, genome build, and position-derived coordinates to obtain the trinucleotide context. For a user's own MAF these coordinates are user-derived; this is the one default path in which user-derived data leaves the device, and it is fully avoidable using bundled/offline context tables or strict-local mode.**

**We also document incidental persistence: the SDK uses the browser Cache API for fetched public resources, so URLs containing identifiers can persist as cache keys, and certain diagnostic paths log identifiers to the console. The SDK does not use localStorage, sessionStorage, IndexedDB, or cookies for user data. Console logging of identifiers is gated behind an explicit debug flag (default off), and cache persistence is disabled under strict-local mode (below).**

**Table A summarizes the contacted endpoints. The complete endpoint audit is provided in the supplementary material.** 

**Table A\. Network endpoints reachable from the SDK and what each transmits.**

| Endpoint / host | Triggering operation | What is sent | User-derived data? |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| mSigPortal API (analysistools.cancer.gov) | Public spectra / reference signature fetch | Study/cancer/strategy/profile filters; sample identifiers when requested | Public identifiers only |
| Genomic Data Commons API (api.gdc.cancer.gov) | Public expression / MAF file fetch | Gene, project, and file identifiers | Public identifiers only |
| UCSC sequence API (api.genome.ucsc.edu) | Online MAF context lookup | Genome build, chromosome, coordinates | Yes (user MAF coordinates), unless offline/bundled context used |
| CDN module hosts (esm.sh, cdn.jsdelivr.net) | Module / runtime / library import | Module GET requests only | No |
| Pyodide / WebR runtime \+ package hosts | Adapter execution | Runtime and package URLs; no spectra in URLs | No |

**We therefore make two distinct claims. Unconditionally, the SDK never transmits user spectra, exposures, quality-control results, or reports. Conditionally — and now achievable with a single configuration flag — a fully local, no-egress workflow including MAF conversion is available via strict-local mode and bundled context tables.**

**We are also explicit about residual trust assumptions, following reviewer concern about browser and supply-chain exposure. A browser-native tool inherits the browser's own attack surface, and the JavaScript ecosystem is subject to supply-chain compromise. To reduce this exposure we pin all third-party runtimes and libraries to fixed versions and verify the integrity of bundled package artifacts (see "Runtime pinning and integrity verification"). We do not claim a formal privacy or security guarantee, and we have accordingly avoided describing the system as providing "private computation" in a cryptographic sense; the contribution is browser-native, client-side computation with a precisely bounded and largely user-controllable data-residency profile.**

### **Runtime pinning and integrity verification**

**To support reproducibility and reduce supply-chain risk, every external runtime and library is loaded at a fixed version rather than a moving tag. The WebR runtime is pinned to v0.6.0, the Pyodide runtime to v0.27.4, and the third-party visualization and parsing libraries to fixed releases (amCharts 5.3.7, pako 2.1.0, PapaParse 5.5.3, alongside the previously pinned UMAP 1.3.3, Plotly 3.5.1, D3 7.9.0, and ml-regression-multivariate-linear 2.0.4). Bundled Pyodide wheels and WebR package artifacts are additionally verified by SHA-256 against a recorded manifest before installation, and installation fails loudly on mismatch; in our test environment 11 Pyodide artifacts and 8 WebR artifacts were verified in this way. We are explicit about the boundary of this protection: bundled package artifacts are integrity-verified, whereas CDN-delivered ECMAScript modules are version-pinned but not Subresource-Integrity-verified, because the SDK's dynamic `import()` and `importScripts` load paths do not carry browser SRI metadata. Pinned adapter runtime versions are recorded in Table 2 and in report provenance; the complete integrity manifest is provided in the supplement.**

### **Strict-local (no-egress) mode**

**The SDK exposes an optional strict-local mode through a single configuration call. When enabled, it hard-disables every code path capable of transmitting user-derived data: the sample-specific mSigPortal spectrum fetch, all Genomic Data Commons gene/project/file calls, and the online UCSC context lookup used in MAF conversion. In this mode, MAF-to-profile conversion uses only row-supplied or bundled/offline context tables and raises a clear, actionable error if a network context lookup would otherwise be required; cache persistence of fetched responses is disabled; and diagnostic logging of identifiers remains off. Native fitting, exploratory extraction, quality control, and report serialization all operate fully in this mode. We verified the guarantee empirically: a strict-mode run comprising NNLS fitting, quality control, exploratory extraction, report generation, and a bundled-context MAF conversion produced zero network requests carrying user-derived data (observed network log empty). A documented strict-local workflow is provided with the SDK.**

### **Core algorithms**

**For readers seeking implementation detail, we summarize the native-tier algorithms; full code is in the repository.**

**Exposure fitting uses non-negative least squares (NNLS): given a sample's observed context vector and a signature catalog matrix, the solver estimates non-negative exposures minimizing reconstruction error, with samples fitted independently. Exploratory de novo extraction uses non-negative matrix factorization (NMF): for a user-supplied sample-by-context matrix and a candidate rank or rank range, the module factorizes the matrix into signature profiles and sample exposures, returns rank-selection diagnostics across the requested range, and matches extracted profiles to a reference catalog by cosine similarity. Uncertainty is estimated by bootstrap: the observed spectrum is resampled by multinomial draws and refit, yielding per-exposure confidence intervals and stability summaries; bootstrap runs serially by default and now also provides opt-in worker-backed paths for single-sample and cohort uncertainty estimation (see "Runtime and bottlenecks"). The quality-control layer computes a panel of evidence fields and combines them, via user-configurable thresholds, into one of three reporting modes (standard, qualified, restricted) together with recommended actions, as detailed in "Quality-controlled review." MAF-to-profile conversion maps mutation rows to single-base, double-base, and insertion/deletion profile types, obtaining trinucleotide context either from the row itself, from a bundled offline context table keyed by genome build, or — in the default online path only — from the UCSC sequence API.**

### External tool orchestration

The adapter layer lets a single browser session execute and compare multiple established mutational-signature tools on shared spectra. Each adapter follows a uniform three-stage contract: an input-preparation stage produces virtual files and a manifest in the target tool's expected matrix orientation; an execution stage runs the tool in the chosen runtime; and an output-parsing stage reimports the tool's signature and exposure tables into SDK matrix objects through a shared parser. All tools therefore see the same context order, and their outputs are directly comparable without post-hoc reconciliation. Each adapter records the targeted package version in provenance, alongside an internal adapter schema version, so downstream consumers can detect schema changes. Four tools execute in-browser today (**Table 2**). **We distinguish these in-browser executable adapters — which run the wrapped tool and return exposures — from handoff bundles, which package inputs for a tool to be run elsewhere.**

**Table 2\.** Mutational-signature packages that execute in-browser through the mSigSDK adapter layer.

| Tool | In-browser runtime | Primary role |
| ----- | ----- | ----- |
| SigProfilerAssignment 1.1.3 | Pyodide v0.27.4  (matrix mode)  | Python known-signature assignment. |
| MuSiCal 1.0.0 | Pyodide v0.27.4  | Python sparse likelihood-based refitting and discovery. |
| deconstructSigs 1.8.0 | WebR v0.6.0  | R-based known-signature decomposition. |
| sigminer 2.3.1 | WebR v0.6.0 (with method-aware solver detection) | R-based supervised fitting and extraction. |

### Exploratory extraction

mSigSDK includes a browser-native implementation of non-negative matrix factorization for exploratory de novo signature extraction. The user provides a sample-by-context matrix and a candidate rank or rank range; the module returns extracted signature profiles, sample-by-signature exposures, rank-selection diagnostics, and matches between extracted signatures and a reference catalog. The intent is screening and instruction rather than production de novo discovery at large multi-cohort scale, which remains the domain of dedicated extraction tools.

### Quality-controlled review

The SDK includes a configurable quality-control layer that converts a panel of evidence fields into a burden-aware reporting mode and recommended actions. Three reporting modes are produced – a standard mode, a qualified mode, and a restricted mode – from separable evidence fields covering mutation burden, context coverage, reconstruction quality (cosine similarity, root-mean-square error, mean absolute error, error norms, and maximum residual), residual structure, bootstrap stability from multinomial resampling of the observed spectrum, threshold sensitivity, signature ambiguity from pairwise signature cosine and nearest-neighbor entropy, catalog sufficiency, and panel or whole-exome sequencing evidence.

**The thresholds that drive each evidence field, and the rules combining them into a reporting mode, are user-configurable. We want to be unambiguous about their status: the shipped defaults are convenience values that we found reasonable on the cohorts described below, chosen to be conservative rather than calibrated against any gold standard, and they are explicitly not community-consensus thresholds. Table B lists the principal evidence fields and their default thresholds. Each evidence field is also emitted as a structured object, so a downstream consumer can either accept the assigned reporting mode as a single trust indicator or apply its own combination rule over the underlying evidence. Because these defaults are uncalibrated, the reporting modes and recommended actions are intended for research use only and are not a clinical or diagnostic determination.**

**Table B. Principal quality-control evidence fields and default thresholds.**

| Evidence area | Default rule | Interpretation |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| Mutation burden | WGS: low \<100; moderate \<1000. Panel/WES: low \<30; moderate \<150. | Low-burden samples receive more cautious reporting. |
| Context coverage | Observed contexts must match the selected profile. | Missing or extra contexts are treated as input-format issues. |
| Reconstruction and residuals | Review when cosine \<0.9, unexplained fraction \>=0.07, or residual-reference cosine \>=0.85. | Poor fit or structured residuals suggest caveated interpretation or catalog review. |
| Exposure-threshold sensitivity | Evaluate cutoffs 0, 1%, 3%, 5%, and 10%; review cosine drops \>=0.02. | Highlights fits whose active signatures change across reporting cutoffs. |
| Bootstrap stability | 200 iterations by default; 500 for stable-interval review; 1000 recommended for publication use. | Reports sampling uncertainty conditional on the observed spectrum and catalog. |
| Signature ambiguity | Review similar signatures at cosine \>=0.9; stronger ambiguity at \>=0.95. | Marks exposure estimates that may redistribute among similar signatures. |
| Catalog sufficiency | Review when unexplained fraction \>=0.12 or catalog cosine \<0.9. | Identifies cases where the reference catalog may not explain observed structure. |

Sensitivity analysis of these thresholds was not completed in this revision; the defaults should therefore be read as configurable review heuristics rather than calibrated decision thresholds.

### Reproducible reports and provenance

Results are serialized under a **proposed, versioned** JSON Schema for mutational-signature analysis reports. Each report records the method used (the fitting solver, the adapter and its targeted package version, or the extraction algorithm), the parameters used for fitting and quality control, the validation outcomes, the quality-control evidence fields, the warnings raised, the recommended actions, descriptors for any figures generated, and a provenance block covering the genome build, reference-context source, public-resource endpoints queried, fetch timestamps, and cache status. **To our knowledge no community schema currently exists for mutational-signature reports; we therefore release the mSigSDK schema as a proposed interchange format rather than an established standard, and offer it for other tools to adopt or extend. The schema carries an explicit version identifier and `$id`, documents required versus optional fields, and ships with a runnable validation command, two passing examples (minimal and full), and one intentionally failing record; a versioning policy distinguishes backward-compatible additions from breaking changes.** Reports may be emitted as structured JSON or as standalone HTML with the JSON embedded. 

### Visualization

The plotting layer utilizes mSigPortal [\[12\]](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?KzVebQ) mutational-spectrum renderers and D3 [\[21\]](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?8noOf9), Plotly [\[22\]](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?qm7q7v), and AMCharts [\[23\]](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?g8IHtN) wrappers, producing over thirty interactive views, including mutation burden summaries, clustered cosine similarity heatmaps, similarity trees, uniform manifold approximation projections, exposure heatmaps, bootstrap confidence intervals, threshold sensitivity plots, and non-negative matrix factorization rank-selection diagnostics. A complete catalog of plot functions is in the documentation.

### **Headless and scripted execution**

**Although the browser is the primary target, the SDK's native-JavaScript core (matrix conversion, NNLS fitting, NMF, quality control, and report serialization) is also invocable headlessly under Node.js, without a browser. The implementation targets Node.js 18 or later; in this revision environment the runnable example was executed under Node v24.13.0 and wrote a schema-valid JSON report after loading a spectrum, fitting it against a catalog, and running quality control. We now provide both a copy-paste browser-console example and a compact Node example, in addition to the fuller headless report-writing script. Browser-only capabilities are DOM/canvas-based rendering and the browser-worker WebR/Pyodide adapter execution paths. Integration with workflow specifications such as the Common Workflow Language, and a dedicated command-line wrapper, are natural extensions of this headless path and are noted as future work.**

## Results

The SDK was assessed across four areas: that it can be loaded and used without installation; that the in-browser adapter layer reproduces local wrapped-tool behavior where local comparators were available; that the internal solvers reproduce established reference implementations; and that it performs well enough for interactive use in common exposure-solve scenarios. 

### Zero-install browser execution

**To demonstrate the zero-install architecture, a complete analysis session was run in a fresh browser tab, recording the time from page-load start to a rendered, exportable report (Figure 2).** The session began with the dynamic module import, retrieved a public PCAWG Lung-AdenoCA single-base substitution-96 spectrum from mSigPortal, fitted it against the mSigPortal COSMIC v3 GRCh37 single-base substitution-96 catalog (67 signatures; 33 active fitted signatures), and produced the corresponding quality-controlled report, an 8,931-byte JSON packet, in 4.993 s end-to-end in the current cold fresh-profile Chrome run. **The within-session step timings are cumulative from page-load start: 4.713 s to import the SDK module graph, 4.969 s once the public spectrum and catalog had also been retrieved, 4.991 s once fitting had completed, and 4.993 s once report generation had completed.** The fit and report steps themselves accounted for only the final tens of milliseconds; most cold-start time was module import and public-input retrieval. No software installation, environment configuration, or manual data download was required from the user. 

![][image2]**Figure 2\.** Zero-install workflow demonstration. Automated in-page timing from a fresh browser-profile demo page: after page load begins, the SDK imports, fetches public PCAWG and COSMIC inputs, fits one single-base substitution-96 spectrum against the mSigPortal COSMIC v3 GRCh37 single-base substitution-96 catalog (67 signatures), and renders a local report in 4.993 s in the current cold Chrome run. **Reported step timings are cumulative from page-load start.** Human time to open a browser or navigate to the page is not included. 

### Fidelity of the in-browser adapter layer against local execution

To establish that the adapter layer faithfully executes wrapped tools rather than silently altering their behavior, we compared browser execution against local reference execution on the revision host. Inputs were 38 PCAWG Lung-AdenoCA single-base substitution-96 spectra fitted against the mSigPortal COSMIC v3 GRCh37 single-base substitution-96 catalog (67 signatures). The deconstructSigs and sigminer WebR adapters reproduced their local R reference outputs exactly (maximum absolute exposure difference 0; root-mean-square error 0; mean and median exposure-vector cosine 1; minimum cosine 0.9999999999999998; top-signature concordance 1 across all 38 samples). The SigProfilerAssignment Pyodide adapter reproduced its Dockerized Python reference exactly by these metrics. The MuSiCal Pyodide adapter also matched its Dockerized Python reference to floating-point roundoff (maximum absolute exposure difference 4.163336342344337e-15; root-mean-square error 2.5021563225878793e-16; mean and median exposure-vector cosine 1; minimum cosine 0.9999999999999997; top-signature concordance 1).

**We emphasize what this comparison does and does not establish. Agreement with the local installation demonstrates adapter fidelity — that the in-browser path faithfully reproduces a wrapped tool's output under matched inputs and options — and not the independent biological validity of the underlying attribution method, which remains the responsibility of each wrapped tool. Full reproducibility records for the completed comparisons are provided in the supplementary material.** 

### Numerical agreement of internal solvers

The non-negative least-squares signature fitting solver matches established Python and R reference implementations to within floating-point tolerance, with a largest absolute exposure difference of 1.65×10−9. The non-negative matrix factorization extraction algorithm achieves a reconstruction error 0.918× that of the scikit-learn reference and recovers matched components with a high median cosine similarity of 0.967. 

### Capability demonstration on a public cohort

**The SDK was demonstrated on** 38 publicly available PCAWG Lung-AdenoCA single-base substitution-96 spectra retrieved through mSigPortal and fitted against the mSigPortal COSMIC v3 GRCh37 single-base substitution-96 catalog (67 signatures), in a single browser session and without server-side computation (Figure 3). **All inputs are public, derived resources accessed through unauthenticated requests; no controlled-access data were used.** SBS4 dominated the cohort, consistent with the known tobacco-associated etiology of lung adenocarcinoma. Fit quality was high across the cohort (median fit cosine 0.997) and remained robust under exposure-cutoff stringency: raising the cutoff from 0 to 5% reduced the mean number of active signatures more than six-fold but degraded the mean reconstruction cosine by less than four percentage points, indicating that most low-exposure calls were numerically minor. Exploratory non-negative matrix factorization on the same cohort selected rank 6, with the extracted components recovering profiles broadly consistent with the dominant fitted signatures. All panels were generated from the same imported matrix in the same browser session and exported in the SDK's report format with full provenance.

**This demonstration is scoped to SBS96 lung-adenocarcinoma fitting against a single COSMIC v3 GRCh37 catalog. Other profile types (DBS, ID, SBS1536), tumor types, sequencing strategies (targeted panel and whole-exome), genome builds, and the MAF-conversion path are supported by the implementation but are not separately validated here; broader validation, including synthetic mixtures with known exposures, is identified as future work.**

A complete walk-through of the workflow – including per-sample evidence cards, bootstrap uncertainty estimation, and the full set of cohort visualizations – is available as a runnable notebook at [https://episphere.github.io/msig/notebooks/viewer.html?notebook=msig-sdk-end-to-end-workflow.onb.html](https://episphere.github.io/msig/notebooks/viewer.html?notebook=msig-sdk-end-to-end-workflow.onb.html).

![][image3]

**Figure 3\. Browser-side public cohort capability summary.** Thirty-eight public PCAWG Lung-AdenoCA SBS96 spectra were fetched from mSigPortal, fitted in the browser against the mSigPortal COSMIC v3 GRCh37 SBS96 catalog with 67 signatures, and summarized as manuscript-scale SDK outputs. Panels show (A) mean normalized COSMIC exposure across the cohort for the dominant fitted signatures, with remaining signatures grouped as Other; (B) cohort quality-control metrics and mutation-burden distribution; (C) exposure-threshold sensitivity, comparing active-signature counts with reconstruction fit across cutoffs; and (D) exploratory rank-6 non-negative matrix factorization, including rank diagnostics and the six extracted SBS96 component profiles.

### Runtime and browser compatibility

### **Runtime and bottlenecks**

**Two distinct workloads must be distinguished when interpreting runtime, because they differ by orders of magnitude and were conflated in the original submission.**

**The first is the exposure solve in isolation. On the benchmark host (Windows, 32 CPUs, 31.2 GB RAM),** warm-start single-sample fit-and-report tasks completed in 1.20 ms median in Chrome, 1.07 ms in Edge, and 2.89 ms in Firefox. Warm-start 120-sample cohort **NNLS solves** completed in 18.18 ms median in Chrome, 17.31 ms in Edge, and 25.28 ms in Firefox. Warm-start 300-sample, 40-signature **solves** completed in 1.75 s median in Chrome, 1.65 s in Edge, and 2.88 s in Firefox. Bootstrap uncertainty with 500 iterations on a single sample completed in 237.9 ms, 232.9 ms, and 238.6 ms median in Chrome, Edge, and Firefox, respectively. Non-negative matrix factorization rank selection followed by rank-4 extraction completed in 3.20 s median in Chrome, 3.05 s in Edge, and 2.27 s in Firefox. These medians are from 20 isolated warm-start repeats per browser; corresponding cold-start rows and stage fields for load, network fetch, module import, runtime initialization, pure-JS compute, serialization, and available heap are retained in the benchmark data files. **These figures measure the named operation only — for the cohort scenarios, the NNLS exposure solve — and explicitly exclude the end-to-end notebook workflow.**

**The second workload is the full end-to-end "fit and review" session as run by the demonstration notebook, which additionally performs full-catalog fitting, threshold-sensitivity analysis, quality-control evidence, all-sample bootstrap uncertainty, report preparation, and plotting.** We did not complete the requested 120- and 500-sample public TCGA Lung-AdenoCA end-to-end notebook benchmark in this revision because the requested public input was not available from tested API queries or local repository artifacts on the Windows host. We therefore do not report a Figure 5 or a measured percentage contribution for bootstrap. The distinction remains important because all-sample bootstrap scales with the number of samples multiplied by the requested bootstrap iterations; for 500 samples and 100 bootstrap iterations, this implies 50,000 NNLS bootstrap solves before plotting or final report review. To address this bottleneck, the revised SDK now includes opt-in worker-backed bootstrap paths: `bootstrapSignatureFitParallel` splits iterations for one sample, `bootstrapCohortSignatureFitParallel` divides cohort samples across workers, and `runCohortFit` uses the cohort worker path when `parallelBootstrap` is enabled. These changes speed the bootstrap stage, but they do not replace the missing public 120- and 500-sample end-to-end notebook benchmark.

**On where the bottleneck sits: the measured exposure-solve workflows are not API-bound; once inputs are available, their runtime is governed by client-side JavaScript computation and browser/runtime startup costs.** For full cohort review with bootstrap enabled, practical scale is governed primarily by client CPU, worker count, and the chosen bootstrap configuration. In synthetic Node.js worker-thread stress tests on the same Windows host, a 500-iteration single-sample bootstrap improved from 1.510 s median serial runtime to 0.845 s with four workers, with median peak resident memory increasing from 116 MiB to 259 MiB. A 120-sample, 25-iteration-per-sample cohort bootstrap improved from 12.276 s median serial runtime to 4.647 s with four workers, with median peak resident memory increasing from 235 MiB to 546 MiB. A 300-sample, 10-iteration-per-sample, 40-signature cohort bootstrap completed in 1.990 s median with four workers. Node heap-cap checks passed at 128 MiB for the single-sample worker test and 256 MiB for the 120-sample worker test, but these are tested lower bounds for synthetic Node workloads, not universal browser system-memory requirements. These stress-test results are summarized in Supplementary Figure S and Table C. The measurements support a conservative recommendation of a modern multi-core desktop or laptop browser for cohort review, with bootstrap iterations reduced or deferred for larger cohorts when interactive latency matters.

All workflows tested, including module loading, public-resource retrieval, native fitting and report generation, D3 visualization, and the Pyodide and WebR adapters, passed on the three browsers tested on the available Windows host (Chrome, Edge, and Firefox). Because the SDK is implemented in JavaScript and browser Web APIs rather than operating-system-specific native code, compatibility is primarily a browser/runtime question; the performance measurements reported here are nevertheless scoped to the tested Windows desktop-browser environment.

![][image4]

**Figure 4\. Browser runtime benchmarks** **(exposure-solve scenarios only).** Warm-start median elapsed time with IQR whiskers across 20 isolated repeats in Chrome, Edge, and Firefox on the benchmark host (Windows, 32 CPUs, 31.2 GB RAM). Scenarios are single-sample non-negative least-squares fit and report; 120-sample cohort **NNLS solve**; 300-sample, 40-signature **solve**; 500-iteration bootstrap uncertainty on a single sample; and non-negative matrix factorization rank selection plus rank-4 extraction. **These scenarios measure the named operation only and exclude the end-to-end notebook workflow characterized in the text.** Cold-start rows and stage decompositions for load, network fetch, module import, runtime initialization, pure-JS compute, serialization, and available heap are retained in the benchmark data files. Log-scale axis keeps fast single-sample operations visible alongside slower workflows. 

## Discussion

The validation results carry **several** substantive implications worth highlighting.

The first concerns the cost of trust in browser-based scientific software. A common objection to browser-native scientific tools is that running computations in a sandboxed WebAssembly or JavaScript environment may silently change their behavior relative to a local installation. The adapter-fidelity results did not show such divergence: deconstructSigs, sigminer, and SigProfilerAssignment reproduced their local reference outputs exactly across 38 samples and a 67-signature catalog, while MuSiCal differed only at double-precision roundoff scale.

The second concerns the asymmetric runtime profile we observed. Browser fitting workflows are fast for exposure-solve tasks at common cohort scales: a warm-start 120-sample cohort **exposure solve** completed in 17-25 ms median across the three tested Windows browsers. The full fit-and-review path is broader than this benchmark because it can include quality-control evidence, threshold review, all-sample bootstrap uncertainty, report preparation, and plotting. We did not complete the requested 120- and 500-sample end-to-end notebook benchmark in this revision, but the current implementation makes the likely slow path clear: all-sample bootstrap scales with the number of samples multiplied by the requested bootstrap iterations. The revised SDK therefore adds opt-in worker-backed bootstrap for both single-sample and cohort settings, and synthetic stress tests show that cohort bootstrap benefits from multi-core execution. At larger scales, the cost of in-browser non-negative matrix factorization also grows noticeably, which is the practical boundary at which in-browser exploration begins to displace, rather than complement, server-side or local analysis, and pairs naturally with handoff to heavier extraction tools when production-scale de novo discovery is the goal.

The third concerns what becomes possible once a mutational-signature analysis can be shared as a single self-contained JSON document with full provenance. The **proposed, versioned** schema makes it possible to embed a complete analysis result – method, parameters, fitted exposures, quality-control evidence, warnings, and the public-resource endpoints from which inputs were retrieved – into a manuscript supplement or a portal record, and to have it consumed and re-rendered by any other tool that recognizes the schema. This is a different kind of reproducibility than scripted pipelines: not a recipe for re-running the analysis, but a self-describing record of what was actually done. **We deliberately frame this as a research-interoperability format and make no claim about clinical or electronic-health-record use, which would require calibration and validation well beyond the scope of this work.** Combined with strict-local execution and offline context sources where needed, this makes the SDK well suited to settings where local review is required but signature-level results need to travel.

**A fourth implication concerns the API-first design itself. Engaging public resources directly through their APIs, rather than bundling a local copy, keeps the SDK lightweight and aligned with FAIR principles, but it makes result reproducibility contingent on the continued availability and stability of those public endpoints — a more than theoretical concern given recent disruptions to public health-data resources. We weigh this explicitly rather than treating the API-first choice as strictly preferable. Three factors mitigate the risk: bundled reference artifacts are SHA-256 verified, so corruption or substitution of those artifacts is detected; report provenance records the endpoints queried and fetch timestamps, so a changed or stale upstream result is at least detectable after the fact; and strict-local mode with bundled context tables allows core workflows to run without live fetches. We are candid about the limit of these mitigations: the SDK does not currently hash-verify arbitrary responses from public APIs, so an upstream API that returns altered data or goes offline remains a genuine reproducibility and availability risk that users should weigh for their use case.**

**A fifth implication concerns who the tool is for and how they reach it. The browser-native, client-side design is motivated chiefly by data residency, and it serves two audiences through two entry points. For developers and automated pipelines, the SDK is callable as a library, including headlessly under Node.js, which is the appropriate interface for scripting and continuous integration. For wet-lab and translational researchers, the developer-console entry point used in our demonstrations is not an appropriate primary interface, and we do not present it as one; a form-driven graphical front-end that wraps the SDK as its primary entry point is the intended interface for that audience and is in active development. We make this division explicit because a single entry point does not suit both audiences.**

Several limitations should be acknowledged. mSigSDK is not a new attribution algorithm and does not replace production-scale de novo extraction or disease-specific validation; users seeking those should run the underlying packages locally. **Our quantitative validation is scoped to SBS96 lung-adenocarcinoma fitting against a single COSMIC v3 GRCh37 catalog; other profile types, tumor types, sequencing strategies, genome builds, and the MAF-conversion path are implemented but not separately validated here.** The in-browser adapter paths depend on compatible WebR and Pyodide package builds, which evolve independently of mSigSDK. Mutation annotation format conversion depends on the correct genome build, coordinate conventions, and reference-context availability; offline deployments should supply project-specific context lookup tables rather than rely on bundled example assets. The default thresholds in the quality-control layer are convenience defaults rather than community-consensus values, and we expect users to tune them to their own catalog, cohort, and downstream use case. **Browser-native execution inherits the browser's attack surface, and CDN-delivered modules are version-pinned but not SRI-verified; we reduce but do not eliminate supply-chain exposure. The SDK does not currently persist results across sessions by design, relying instead on explicit report export.** The present performance evaluation covered Chrome, Edge, and Firefox on Windows; other browser/runtime combinations may differ in worker scheduling, memory ceilings, and WebAssembly performance and should be benchmarked separately when those environments are the deployment target.

Several directions are planned for future development. **Bootstrap scheduling will be tuned further, including adaptive selection of samples for uncertainty estimation when cohort-scale all-sample bootstrap is unnecessary for the review question.** Browser/runtime performance will be broadened beyond the Windows Chrome/Edge/Firefox measurements reported here. **Optional browser-native persistence (for example via IndexedDB or the File System Access API) is planned so that results can survive across sessions without reintroducing an installation step.** The adapter layer will be expanded to include additional established mutational-signature tools as compatible WebR and Pyodide builds become available. The JSON Schema for mutational-signature reports will be submitted for community review, with the goal of establishing it as an interoperability standard that tools outside mSigSDK can adopt or extend. **A command-line wrapper and Common Workflow Language integration, building on the existing headless Node.js path, are planned to support automated pipelines.** The quality-control default thresholds will be revisited as the SDK is applied to additional cancer types and sequencing panel configurations, with the aim of publishing a companion set of community-informed defaults. Finally, a **form-driven graphical front-end** is planned **as the primary entry point for non-developer users,** while preserving the SDK's zero-install **and** data-residency guarantees.

## Conclusions

mSigSDK is a browser-native, zero-install, FAIR-aligned JavaScript software development kit for mutational-signature analysis. It orchestrates four established mutational-signature packages – SigProfilerAssignment, MuSiCal, deconstructSigs, and sigminer – through a uniform adapter layer; provides exploratory non-negative matrix factorization and opt-in worker-backed bootstrap uncertainty in JavaScript; scores fits against a configurable, burden-aware quality-control evidence panel; and serializes results under a **proposed, versioned** JSON Schema with full provenance. The SDK loads without installation, **and never transmits user spectra, exposures, quality-control results, or reports off the device; an optional strict-local mode provides a verified no-egress workflow for users who require it.** In the current cold fresh-profile Chrome walk-through, a full single-sample browser analysis against a 67-signature COSMIC catalog reached report-ready state in 4.993 s, while warm-start cohort exposure solves completed in milliseconds to seconds depending on cohort size and browser. Local-reference adapter fidelity was confirmed for all four wrapped tools on the 38-sample SBS96 benchmark. It is accessible to anyone working with mutational signatures – including computational analysts, translational researchers, methods developers, and educators – without the configuration overhead of conventional installations. 

## Availability and requirements

**Project name:** mSigSDK

**Project home page:** [https://episphere.github.io/msig/](https://episphere.github.io/msig/)

**Documentation:** [https://episphere.github.io/msig/docs/](https://episphere.github.io/msig/docs/)

**Example notebooks:** [https://episphere.github.io/msig/notebooks/viewer.html](https://episphere.github.io/msig/notebooks/viewer.html)

**Operating system(s):** Platform independent (tested on Windows with Chrome, Edge, and Firefox)

**Programming language:** JavaScript (ECMAScript modules)

**Current version:** 0.3.0; final submission snapshot to be identified by repository tag or release.

**Other requirements:** A modern standards-compliant browser with ECMAScript module and Web Worker support, or Node.js version 18 or later for **headless/local** execution (the example was run in this revision under Node v24.13.0); WebR **(pinned v0.6.0)** for the in-browser deconstructSigs and sigminer adapters; Pyodide **(pinned v0.27.4)** for the in-browser SigProfilerAssignment and MuSiCal adapters; internet access for queries to mSigPortal, The Cancer Genome Atlas, the Genomic Data Commons, and the UCSC Genome Browser when those public resources are used **(not required in strict-local mode with bundled context tables)**

**License:** MIT

**Any restrictions to use by non-academics:** None

## List of abbreviations

API: application programming interface. COSMIC: Catalogue of Somatic Mutations in Cancer. **CWL: Common Workflow Language.** DBS: double-base substitution. GDC: Genomic Data Commons. ID: insertion or deletion. JSON: JavaScript Object Notation. MAF: mutation annotation format. NMF: non-negative matrix factorization. NNLS: non-negative least squares. PCAWG: Pan-Cancer Analysis of Whole Genomes. QC: quality control. SBS: single-base substitution. **SDK: software development kit. SRI: Subresource Integrity.** TCGA: The Cancer Genome Atlas. WES: whole-exome sequencing. WGS: whole-genome sequencing. 

## Declarations

#### **Ethics approval and consent to participate**

Not applicable.

#### **Consent for publication**

Not applicable.

#### **Availability of data and materials**

The mSigSDK source code and example workflows are publicly available at https://github.com/episphere/msig. The data supporting the findings of this study are available from mSigPortal at [https://analysistools.cancer.gov/mutational-signatures/\#/](https://analysistools.cancer.gov/mutational-signatures/#/). Notebooks demonstrating example use of the SDK are available at [https://episphere.github.io/msig/notebooks/viewer.html](https://episphere.github.io/msig/notebooks/viewer.html). **The supplementary material includes the full network-endpoint audit, the runtime version and integrity manifest, strict-local no-egress evidence, benchmark result files, and the adapter-fidelity reproducibility records described in Results.** 

### **Note on prior versions**

Compared with the prior public preprint, this revision adds an explicit data-residency and threat model, runtime pinning and integrity verification, strict-local no-egress mode, corrected benchmark scoping, a hardened versioned report schema, headless Node.js execution, and narrowed validation claims matched to the regenerated evidence.

#### **Competing interests**

A.G., T.Z., Y.C.M., M.T.L., B.P., K.C., J.B., and J.S.A. declare no competing interests.

#### **Funding**

This work was funded by the National Cancer Institute Intramural Research Program (DCEG/Episphere number 10901). The funder had no role in the design of the study; in the collection, analysis, or interpretation of the data; or in the writing of the manuscript.

#### **Authors' contributions**

A.G. and  J.S.A conceived and designed the study. A.G. developed the mSigSDK, conducted the implementation and testing, created the figures, and wrote the manuscript. J.S.A. provided project direction and technical guidance. T.Z., Y.C.M., M.T.L., B.P., K.C., and J.B. provided feedback, advice, and reviewed the software and manuscript drafts. All authors reviewed and approved the final manuscript.

#### **Acknowledgements**

Not applicable.
